title: Air-fryer aubergine
subtitle: Miso-glazed aubergine slices cooked until tender and caramelised.
excerpt: Thick rounds of aubergine brushed with a sweet-salty miso glaze and cooked in the air fryer until collapsed, caramelised and intensely flavoured. A Japanese-inspired side or light main. Serves two.
sourceNotes: Miso-glazed aubergine (nasu dengaku). Grilled miso-glazed aubergine is a traditional Japanese dish, documented in cookbooks from the Edo period. The combination of fermented miso paste, mirin and sake or rice wine creates a lacquer-like glaze that caramelises under high heat. The air fryer replicates the intense grilled heat of traditional Japanese broiling methods.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The glaze can be made 3 days ahead and refrigerated.

content.text: Score the cut surface of the aubergine in a cross-hatch pattern before applying the glaze. The scoring allows the heat to penetrate and the glaze to work into the flesh. Do not slice the aubergine too thin; rounds of at least 2 cm hold their shape and develop the correct texture.

Mix {{miso-white}} of white miso, {{mirin}} of mirin, {{soy-sauce-light}} of soy sauce and {{granulated-sugar}} of sugar into a smooth paste. Score the cut surfaces of {{aubergine}} of aubergine rounds in a cross-hatch. Brush generously with the glaze. Air-fry at 200°C for 12 to 15 minutes until the flesh is tender and the glaze is caramelised and slightly charred at the edges. Scatter with sesame seeds and spring onion if using.

items.cause: Cut too thin, or cooked for too short a time.
items.fix: Rounds must be at least 2 cm thick. Aubergine needs time to cook through; increase to 15 minutes if still firm.

items.symptom: Glaze burning before aubergine is cooked
items.cause: Too much sugar in the glaze, or air fryer running hot.
items.fix: Reduce the sugar to half a teaspoon. Miso caramelises quickly; check at 10 minutes.

items.symptom: Flesh dry and spongy rather than yielding
items.cause: Aubergine not salted to draw out moisture first.
items.fix: For a more reliable result, salt the rounds for 20 minutes, rinse and dry before glazing. This draws out moisture and improves the texture.

content.text: Nasu dengaku, the Japanese original, is served at izakayas and kaiseki restaurants as a single elegant dish. At home it is a quick, filling side or light dinner with rice. The miso glaze is one of the most effective vegetable seasonings in any cuisine: salty, sweet and deeply savoury.
